about.seo_title: About the Project – Learning Brains Erasmus+
about.seo_description: Discover the goals, focus areas, and target groups of Learning Brains: an Erasmus+ KA220-VET project bridging artificial intelligence and vocational training in the European manufacturing sector.
about.title: About the Project
about.background_text: The rapid evolution of industrial processes, driven by digitalization and automation, is creating an increasing need for workforce reskilling across Europe. Traditional training approaches often fail to address the specific, contextual learning needs of workers who must adapt to new technologies and processes while remaining productive. Learning Brains addresses this challenge by developing integrated work-based learning systems that combine hands-on workplace experience with structured educational approaches.
objectives_list: Develop innovative work-based learning methodologies tailored for industrial reskilling
Create AI-based tools to support personalized learning paths in work environments
Foster cooperation between VET providers and industrial partners across Europe
Produce open educational resources for industrial reskilling accessible to a wide audience
Contribute to the European policy dialogue on modernizing vocational education and training
target_list: VET providers and trainers in industrial sectors
HR managers and training coordinators in industry
Policy makers in vocational education and employment

partners.seo_description: Meet the 6 European organizations from Spain, Italy, Slovakia, Austria, and Portugal collaborating on the Erasmus+ Learning Brains project about AI and industrial vocational training.

partners.title: Our Partners
partners.subtitle: Learning Brains is a transnational partnership bringing together industrial associations, business support agencies, and training experts from across Europe.
descriptions: A leading industrial federation representing over 800 companies in the metal sector, committed to innovation and training.
The service company of Confindustria Veneto, specializing in training, innovation, and organizational development for regional companies.
Representing the interests of the Styrian business community and providing extensive educational and training support services.
Experts in the design and implementation of innovative pedagogical methodologies and digital learning environments.
The oldest specialized non-profit organization for supporting small and medium-sized enterprises in Slovakia.
Dedicated to human resource development and innovative training solutions for the modern job market.

items_list.title: First Learning Brains Newsletter
items_list.description: We are excited to share the first edition of the Learning Brains project newsletter, where we present the initial steps of this Erasmus+ KA220-VET initiative.
items_list._source_title: First Learning Brains Newsletter
items_list._source_desc: We are excited to share the first edition of the Learning Brains project newsletter, where we present the initial steps of this Erasmus+ KA220-VET initiative.
items_list.content:
We are excited to share the first edition of the Learning Brains project newsletter, where we present the initial steps of this Erasmus+ KA220-VET initiative focused on the use of Artificial Intelligence to improve on-the-job learning and industrial reskilling processes.
This first newsletter highlights some of the key milestones achieved during the first months of the project, including the successful Kick-off Meeting held in Bilbao, where project partners came together to define the strategic roadmap, collaboration dynamics and upcoming activities for the next two years.
The newsletter also introduces the main objectives of the project and the collaborative work that will be carried out by partners from Spain, Italy, Portugal, Austria and Slovakia to develop innovative AI-based training methodologies and tools tailored to industrial environments and SMEs.
In addition, readers will find information about the launch of the project’s communication channels, the first dissemination actions, and the upcoming activities linked to research, focus groups, interviews and the development of the Learning Brains Training Programme and Toolkit.
Through this newsletter series, we aim to keep stakeholders, companies, VET providers and the wider community informed about the project’s progress, results and opportunities for participation.
Stay tuned and follow the Learning Brains journey towards smarter, more flexible and more human-centred workplace learning systems.

impact.strategy_text: Our strategy focuses on reaching key stakeholders across Europe to ensure that project results are widely known and utilized. We use a multi-channel approach including this digital hub, sectoral social media, regional industrial events, and transnational workshops.
impact.impact_text: The Learning Brains project expects to contribute to the modernization of VET systems by providing validated tools for industrial reskilling. Impact will be observed in enhanced adaptability of industrial workers to technological changes and increased efficiency of workplace training methodologies.
impact.note: Note: Project impacts are currently described non-quantitatively as implementation is in its initial phases.

items_list.title: AI-Powered Learning: How Artificial Intelligence Is Transforming Industrial Training
items_list.description: AI-driven learning tools boost knowledge transfer, efficiency, and innovation across the industrial sector.
items_list._source_title: AI-Powered Learning: How Artificial Intelligence Is Transforming Industrial Training
items_list._source_desc: AI-driven learning tools boost knowledge transfer, efficiency, and innovation across the industrial sector.
items_list.content:
AI-driven learning tools boost knowledge transfer, efficiency, and innovation across the industrial sector.

As industries face ever-faster technological change, continuous learning has become a critical element of competitiveness. Employees must constantly adapt to new machines, processes, and digital workflows. Traditional training models, however, often fall short—they are time-consuming, costly, and rarely customized to individual needs. Artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping this landscape by enabling a new era of dynamic and individualized learning in the workplace.
AI-powered learning systems use data and algorithms to analyze how employees learn, recognize gaps, and personalize content in real time. Instead of one-size-fits-all programs, workers receive guidance and learning materials tailored to their roles and current skill levels. A maintenance technician, for example, might receive instant safety reminders or troubleshooting tutorials directly on a smart device while servicing equipment—based on his previous training data and the task at hand.
Personalization is one of the most powerful benefits of AI in workplace learning. Intelligent platforms identify learning patterns, recommend relevant modules, and adjust to each user’s progress. This increases engagement, shortens learning time, and delivers measurable results. At the organizational level, data analytics make it possible to track workforce skill development and pinpoint specific areas for improvement.
Scalability is another major advantage. Whereas traditional training requires physical sessions and interruptions to the workflow, AI-driven learning tools integrate smoothly into daily operations—accessible anytime via mobile devices, tablets, or AR glasses. For instance, companies like Siemens already use digital learning platforms to deliver adaptive, real-world simulations for apprentices and technical staff.
AI also supports knowledge retention within aging workforces. By capturing expertise and making it digitally accessible, companies can preserve know-how even as experienced staff retire. In this sense, AI becomes not merely a facilitator for learning but a long-term guardian of organizational knowledge.
Ultimately, AI marks the shift from static training to continuous, personalized skill development. For industrial enterprises, embracing AI-driven learning is an investment in resilience and innovation—helping teams learn smarter, faster, and better prepared for the future.

items_list.title: AI and jobs: the real challenge is not technology, but people
items_list.description: AI is rapidly reshaping jobs, increasing demand for new skills and continuous learning. The key challenge is helping people adapt to work alongside these technologies.
items_list._source_title: AI and jobs: the real challenge is not technology, but people
items_list._source_desc: AI is rapidly reshaping jobs, increasing demand for new skills and continuous learning. The key challenge is helping people adapt to work alongside these technologies.
items_list.content:
Recent news has once again brought a critical issue into focus: the real impact of artificial intelligence on employment. From layoffs in major tech companies to growing concerns among economists about job polarization, one thing is clear, the labor market is evolving faster than ever.
Some analysts warn that AI could disproportionately affect mid-level roles, potentially reshaping the structure of the workforce and increasing inequality. At the same time, companies are reorganizing their operations, leveraging AI tools to improve efficiency and reduce reliance on traditional roles.
This transformation is not limited to the tech sector. Across Europe, industries are undergoing a deep shift driven by automation, data, and AI adoption. As these technologies become more embedded in daily operations, the demand for new skills is accelerating.
In this context, the real challenge is not whether AI will replace certain tasks, but how we prepare people to work alongside it. Organizations are increasingly recognizing the need for more agile and responsive learning models, ones that are closely connected to the workplace and capable of addressing skills gaps in real time.
At the same time, collaborative initiatives across Europe are exploring how to better align education, training, and industry needs. By bringing together companies, training providers, and technology experts, these ecosystems aim to create more effective and sustainable approaches to workforce development.
Ultimately, AI is not only transforming how we work, but also how we learn. Continuous, work-based, and personalized learning is quickly becoming a strategic priority for organizations that want to remain competitive in a rapidly changing environment.

items_list.title: Kick-off Meeting in Bilbao launches the Learning BRAINS European collaboration.
items_list.description: The city of Bilbao recently hosted the Kick-off Meeting of the Erasmus+ project Learning BRAINS – Integrated On-the-job Learning Systems for Industrial Reskilling, officially marking the beginning of an exciting two-year European collaboration focused on innovation, AI-based learning and industrial reskilling
items_list._source_title: Kick-off Meeting in Bilbao launches the Learning BRAINS European collaboration.
items_list._source_desc: The city of Bilbao recently hosted the Kick-off Meeting of the Erasmus+ project Learning BRAINS – Integrated On-the-job Learning Systems for Industrial Reskilling, officially marking the beginning of an exciting two-year European collaboration focused on innovation, AI-based learning and industrial reskilling
items_list.content:
The city of Bilbao recently hosted the Kick-off Meeting of the Erasmus+ project Learning BRAINS – Integrated On-the-job Learning Systems for Industrial Reskilling, officially marking the beginning of an exciting two-year European collaboration focused on innovation, AI-based learning and industrial reskilling.
The meeting brought together representatives from all partner organisations involved in the consortium, combining both onsite and online participation in a dynamic and collaborative atmosphere. The consortium is coordinated by [Federacion Vizcaina de Empresas del Metal](https://www.fvem.es) and includes the participation of [Confindustria Veneto SIAV S.r.l](https://www.siav.net/wp/), [Room466](https://room466.at/en/home-english/), [Slovak Business Agency](https://www.sbagency.sk/), [Sparkling Intuition](https://sparkling-intuition.eu/) and [Media Creativa 2020, S.L.](https://mediacreativa.eu/).

![Kick-off Meeting in Bilbao - Consortium Partners](images/news/bilbaokickoff.jpg)

Beyond the formal agenda, the meeting provided a valuable opportunity for partners to get to know each other better, exchange experiences and build the foundations for a strong and cooperative working relationship. The diversity of profiles within the consortium — from business associations and chambers of commerce to training providers and technology specialists — created a very enriching environment for discussion and idea sharing.
During the sessions, partners aligned on the project’s objectives, implementation roadmap, communication procedures and management structure. Special attention was given to the coordination mechanisms that will support the collaboration throughout the project lifetime, including work planning, quality assurance procedures, dissemination strategies and transnational cooperation methods.
The consortium also reviewed the structure of the project work packages and the responsibilities assigned to each partner organisation. The project is organised around four main work packages covering project management, communication and dissemination activities, the development of the Learning BRAINS Training Programme, and the creation of the Learning BRAINS Toolkit for the implementation of AI-based on-the-job training systems.
One of the most interesting discussions during the meeting focused on the growing impact of Artificial Intelligence in vocational education and training within industrial SMEs. Partners shared perspectives on the challenges companies currently face regarding workforce upskilling and reskilling, as well as the opportunities that AI can offer to make training more flexible, personalised and accessible in real workplace environments.
The meeting also allowed the consortium to define the first operational steps for the initial research and mapping activities foreseen in the project. These activities will focus on identifying AI tools, innovative practices and successful case studies related to on-the-job learning and industrial training across Europe.
Learning BRAINS aims to support HR managers, training managers, company trainers and industrial SMEs by providing practical resources and innovative methodologies to design and implement AI-based on-the-job training systems. The project will deliver two main results: a multilingual Training Programme and an interactive Toolkit offering practical guidance, case studies and AI resources adapted to industrial training contexts.
The Kick-off Meeting confirmed the strong commitment of all consortium members to developing high-quality and impactful project results, while reinforcing the importance of European cooperation to address current industrial and digital transformation challenges.
With motivation, shared expertise and a clear common vision, the Learning BRAINS partnership begins its journey towards supporting more resilient, innovative and human-centred approaches to industrial reskilling across Europe.

items_list.title: From Training to Real Skills: Why Traditional Workplace Learning Is No Longer Enough
items_list.description: Rapid tech advances require shifting traditional training to continuous, AI-backed, on-the-job learning. Initiatives like Learning Brains lead this transformation to ensure workforce readiness for the future.
items_list._source_title: From Training to Real Skills: Why Traditional Workplace Learning Is No Longer Enough
items_list._source_desc: Rapid tech advances require shifting traditional training to continuous, AI-backed, on-the-job learning. Initiatives like Learning Brains lead this transformation to ensure workforce readiness for the future.
items_list.content:
In today’s industrial landscape, change is happening faster than ever. Automation, digitalisation, artificial intelligence, and evolving production systems are transforming how companies operate. As industries adapt, one challenge becomes increasingly urgent: how can workers continuously develop the skills needed to keep pace with change?
Traditional workplace training methods are no longer enough.
For decades, employee training often relied on one-off seminars, static presentations, or classroom-based sessions disconnected from real workplace situations. While these approaches still have value, they often struggle to deliver lasting impact in fast-changing industrial environments. Employees may gain theoretical knowledge, but applying it effectively in real-world settings remains a challenge.
Modern industries require a different approach — one that prioritises continuous learning, practical application, and adaptability.
This is where on-the-job learning becomes essential. Instead of separating training from daily work, employees learn while performing real tasks, solving practical challenges, and developing skills directly relevant to their roles. Learning becomes embedded into everyday workflows, making it more engaging, efficient, and impactful.
At the same time, artificial intelligence (AI) is opening new possibilities for workplace education. AI-powered learning tools can support more personalised training experiences, adapting content to employees’ skill levels, learning pace, and professional needs. This helps companies move away from “one-size-fits-all” training models toward smarter and more targeted learning solutions.
For industrial SMEs in particular, this shift is increasingly important. Smaller companies often face limited resources for training while also experiencing strong pressure to remain competitive in rapidly changing markets. Flexible, workplace-based learning approaches supported by digital and AI-driven tools can help bridge this gap.
The Learning Brains project responds directly to these challenges by exploring innovative ways to integrate AI into workplace learning and industrial reskilling. By supporting collaboration between vocational education providers, industry partners, and training professionals across Europe, the project aims to strengthen workforce readiness for the future of industry.
Because in the modern industrial world, learning is no longer something that happens occasionally — it must become part of everyday work.

items_list.title: AI as an Ally in the Digital Transition for Adults
items_list.description: The skills demanded by the labour market change faster than traditional education systems can keep up with, and adults face a double challenge: adapting to existing technologies while simultaneously preparing for those that are still emerging.
items_list._source_title: AI as an Ally in the Digital Transition for Adults
items_list._source_desc: The skills demanded by the labour market change faster than traditional education systems can keep up with, and adults face a double challenge: adapting to existing technologies while simultaneously preparing for those that are still emerging.
items_list.content:
We live in an era where digital transformation is advancing at an unprecedented pace. The skills demanded by the labour market change faster than traditional education systems can keep up with, and adults face a double challenge: adapting to existing technologies while simultaneously preparing for those that are still emerging.
The numbers tell a stark story. According to the World Bank, approximately 2.6 billion people still live without access to the internet. Within the European Union alone, nearly half of all adults lack sufficient digital skills to navigate today's digital environment comfortably. At the same time, artificial intelligence (AI), cybersecurity, and data science have become highly valued and increasingly indispensable competencies.
There is a widespread assumption that older adults find it harder to learn digital skills. However, scientific evidence challenges this stereotype: people retain their cognitive capacity as they age, and learning is possible at any stage of life.
What genuinely hinders digital learning in adults is not biology, but contextual and emotional factors:
* Lack of access to adequate, level-appropriate training
* Technology anxiety and fear of making mistakes in digital environments
* Negative prior experiences with technology or formal education systems
* Lack of time and intrinsic motivation
* Training content that is misaligned with adults' real-life and professional contexts
One of the most significant advances AI brings to adult education is the ability to personalise each learner's journey. Adaptive learning systems can identify a user's current skill level, detect specific gaps, and propose content tailored to their pace, learning style, and professional context. Unlike rigid traditional courses, an AI-powered platform can offer a construction worker practical exercises focused on digital tools relevant to their sector, while recommending an accountant pathways centred on financial software or process automation.
AI-based virtual assistants function as personal tutors that are permanently available. They can answer questions in natural language, explain concepts simply, provide immediate feedback on exercises, and monitor a learner's progress without judgement. This continuous availability is especially relevant for adults who study outside working hours or who are reluctant to expose their difficulties in group settings. AI creates a safe space to learn at one's own pace.
Many adults with low qualifications also face challenges with basic literacy. AI tools with text-to-speech, automatic translation, and language simplification capabilities can make access to digital knowledge more inclusive, reaching populations that traditional systems fail to serve.
Digital transition cannot be achieved through technology alone: it requires people who are equipped to teach others. AI can support trainers themselves, helping them create adapted pedagogical materials, monitor cohort progress, and identify who needs additional support. What is at stake is whether this transition will be inclusive — whether it will reach all adults, regardless of age, educational background, geographic location, or economic situation — or whether it will deepen existing social divides.
Artificial intelligence, when applied thoughtfully, has the potential to be the great equaliser of this transformation. It can make learning more accessible, more personalised, more effective, and more motivating for the adults who most need support on their digital journey.
But technology alone is not enough. Political will, public investment, cross-sector coordination, and above all a people-centred vision are required — one that places human needs, fears, and enormous learning potential at its core.

items_list.title: The Brain That Learns by Doing: Driving Industrial Reskilling and Upskilling Through AI and VR
items_list.description: The modern industrial sector is facing a double-edged challenge: a severe skills gap and an unprecedented pace of technological automation. To remain competitive, enterprises cannot just hire new talent—they must transform their existing workforce. This has placed Industrial Upskilling (advancing current skills to match new tech) and Reskilling (training workers for entirely new roles) at the top of the corporate agenda.
items_list._source_title: The Brain That Learns by Doing: Driving Industrial Reskilling and Upskilling Through AI and VR
items_list._source_desc: The modern industrial sector is facing a double-edged challenge: a severe skills gap and an unprecedented pace of technological automation. To remain competitive, enterprises cannot just hire new talent—they must transform their existing workforce. This has placed Industrial Upskilling (advancing current skills to match new tech) and Reskilling (training workers for entirely new roles) at the top of the corporate agenda.
items_list.content:
The modern industrial sector is facing a double-edged challenge: a severe skills gap and an unprecedented pace of technological automation. To remain competitive, enterprises cannot just hire new talent—they must transform their existing workforce. This has placed Industrial Upskilling (advancing current skills to match new tech) and Reskilling (training workers for entirely new roles) at the top of the corporate agenda.

Traditional training methods fail to meet the speed and depth this transition demands. To overcome this bottleneck, forward-thinking organizations are turning to a powerful technological alliance: Virtual Reality (VR) and Artificial Intelligence (AI). Together, they are shifting industrial training from passive content consumption to experiential, brain-optimized learning.

## Accelerating the Industrial Transition: Immersive Body, Adaptive Mind
If VR provides the physical environment (the "body"), AI acts as the cognitive engine (the "mind"). In industrial settings, VR triggers what neuroscientists call spatial presence—making the brain perceive a simulated factory floor or high-risk scenario as real. AI then steps in to personalize the journey, creating the ultimate framework for rapid skill acquisition:
* Adaptive Pathways for Upskilling: Upskilling requires building on a worker’s existing knowledge. AI embedded in VR headsets tracks a technician's behavior in real time—monitoring gaze, decision-making speed, and precision. If an experienced operator is adapting well to a new automated assembly process, the AI accelerates the module. If they struggle with a specific digital interface, the AI adjusts the simulation dynamically, optimizing the cognitive load for efficient upskilling.
* A Risk-Free Environment for Fast Reskilling: Reskilling often means shifting a worker from low-risk tasks to complex, high-risk operations (such as maintenance of automated robotics or chemical handling). The human brain learns best through trial and error, but in industry, errors cost lives and millions in downtime. VR and AI create a psychologically safe "space to fail." Workers can make critical mistakes a thousand times in a simulated environment. The AI analyzes the root cause of the failure and guides the worker through repetitive practice, building the procedural and muscle memory required for their new role before they ever touch real machinery.
* Bridging the Human-Machine Gap: As industries implement advanced automation, cognitive flexibility and rapid troubleshooting under pressure become vital. Powered by Large Language Models (LLMs), AI-driven avatars in VR can simulate high-pressure industrial crises or collaborative engineering environments, allowing workers to hone these complex cognitive and communication skills naturally.

## Breaking the Cost Barrier: Democratizing Innovation
Historically, the adoption of virtual reality was constrained by prohibitive hardware costs and complex infrastructure. Today, the economic barrier has collapsed. The emergence of high-performance, standalone enterprise headsets has democratized access for businesses of all sizes:
   * Entry-Level Deployment: Scalable, independent devices like the Meta Quest 3S enter the market at an accessible price point of $300 to $350, making full-fleet deployments viable for small and medium enterprises (SMEs).
   * Mid-Tier Operations: All-in-one mixed reality setups like the Meta Quest 3 (around $500 to $600) deliver advanced processing and color passthrough without requiring external computing hardware.
   * High-End Enterprise: Robust, business-dedicated systems featuring advanced eye and hand tracking—such as the HTC VIVE Focus Vision (around $1,200 to $1,500) — provide high-fidelity safety simulations that cost a fraction of real-world equipment downtime.
This democratization means that implementing an AI-driven VR training program is no longer an exclusive luxury for tech giants, but a financially viable and highly scalable strategy for any modern workforce.
## From Theory to the Factory Floor: A Framework for Implementation
To successfully harness this neuroeducational power, organizations should avoid treating VR and AI as mere novelty gadgets. Implementation requires a strategic, three-phased approach:
   1. Map the Skills Gap (The AI Audit): Before deploying hardware, companies must use data analytics to identify which industrial processes yield the highest error rates or safety risks. This data defines the baseline for the training modules.
   2. Build the Safe Sandbox (The VR Environment): Develop or acquire modular VR simulations of specific machinery or protocols. The focus here should be on high-fidelity physics—ensuring that virtual switches, weights, and tools mimic their real-world counterparts to guarantee accurate muscle-memory transfer.
   3. Deploy the Cognitive Layer (The AI Tutor): Integrate adaptive AI algorithms that monitor user performance. This layer ensures that the training scales automatically, providing a personalized mentor for every single employee without draining the time of senior human supervisors.

## A Neurodidactic Triumph for Future Industries
Fusing AI and VR for industrial reskilling and upskilling represents the pinnacle of modern neurodidactics. By stimulating the limbic system through immersive experience, this methodology achieves up to four times higher retention rates than traditional classroom training.
As we look toward the future of work, the line between training and actual execution will continue to blur. We are moving toward a paradigm where a worker’s upskilling journey doesn't end when they take off the headset. The cognitive pathways trained in the virtual world—honed by adaptive AI and solidified by immersive experience—become the exact tools that drive innovation on the real-world factory floor.
By respecting the natural mechanics of how the human brain processes, retains, and applies knowledge, the fusion of AI and VR does something extraordinary: it ensures that as industries become smarter and more automated, the human workforce evolves right alongside them, fully equipped to lead the next industrial frontier.

items_list.title: The Changing Role of Vocational Trainers
items_list.description: Highlights how AI supports learning and development, while people remain crucial for guidance, reflection, and skill application.
items_list._source_title: The Changing Role of Vocational Trainers
items_list._source_desc: Highlights how AI supports learning and development, while people remain crucial for guidance, reflection, and skill application.
items_list.content:
## From Knowledge Providers to Learning Facilitators: How the Role of Vocational Trainers Is Changing
A technician encounters a problem on the shop floor. Instead of searching through manuals or waiting for the next training session, they ask an AI tool. Within seconds, they receive an explanation, a step-by-step guide and additional learning resources tailored to their needs.
If knowledge has become instantly accessible, what role do vocational trainers still play?
It is a question many organisations are beginning to ask as AI becomes increasingly integrated into workplace learning. Yet the answer is not that vocational trainers are becoming less important. In fact, the opposite is true.
As access to information becomes easier, the ability to transform information into competence becomes more valuable than ever.
## Access to knowledge is no longer the main challenge
For decades, training largely focused on transferring knowledge from experts to employees. Today, AI can support many of these tasks by generating explanations, adapting content to different skill levels and providing immediate answers to specific questions.
However, access to information does not automatically lead to learning.
Employees still need to understand how new knowledge applies to their work, how to use it effectively, how to solve problems with it and how to adapt it to real-life situations. These are processes that require guidance, reflection and experience.
The real challenge for organisations is therefore no longer providing information. It is helping employees develop the skills and confidence to apply that information in practice.
## From knowledge providers to learning facilitators
This shift is fundamentally changing the role of vocational trainers.
Rather than acting primarily as knowledge providers, trainers are increasingly becoming learning facilitators. Their role is to create the conditions that enable continuous learning, support employees in navigating complex information and help them translate knowledge into action and adapt it into real-life-situations.
This change is particularly relevant in industrial environments, where technological innovation is accelerating and skill requirements are constantly evolving. New tools, new processes and new technologies demand learning that is faster, more flexible and more closely connected to daily work.
Employees cannot rely solely on occasional training courses. They need ongoing support that helps them learn while working.
This is where trainers make a critical difference.
## Why human guidance becomes more valuable in the age of AI
The widespread availability of AI does not reduce the need for vocational trainers. Instead, it allows them to focus on the aspects of learning that create the greatest value.
AI can provide answers. Trainers help employees ask the right questions.
AI can generate content. Trainers help employees understand what is relevant.
AI can personalise information. Trainers help employees develop judgement, problem-solving abilities and confidence.
In other words, technology can support learning, but it cannot replace the human elements that make learning meaningful and effective.
The more organisations adopt AI-based learning solutions, the more important it becomes to have professionals who can guide learning processes, foster engagement and ensure that development activities align with organisational goals.
## A strategic opportunity for companies
For companies, this transformation represents more than a change in training methods. It is an opportunity to rethink how workforce development is organised.
Learning facilitators can help create more agile and responsive learning environments where employees acquire new skills when they need them, not weeks or months later. They can support personalised learning pathways, encourage knowledge sharing and help organisations adapt more quickly to technological and market changes.
This is particularly important at a time when many industries are facing skill shortages, rapid digital transformation and increasing pressure to reskill and upskill their workforce.
Companies that succeed will not necessarily be those with the most advanced technology. They will be those that combine technology with effective learning strategies and human support.
## The future of workplace learning
The rise of AI is not making trainers obsolete. It is redefining their contribution.
As information becomes abundant, guidance becomes essential. As knowledge becomes easier to access, learning becomes more valuable. And as AI takes over routine tasks, trainers can focus on what matters most: helping people grow, adapt and perform in a changing world.
The future of workplace learning is therefore not about replacing trainers with technology. It is about empowering vocational trainers to become architects of learning, enabling employees and organisations to unlock the full potential of AI-supported development.

legal.seo_title: Legal Notice – Privacy & Cookies | Learning Brains
legal.seo_description: Privacy policy, cookie information, and transparency notice of the Learning Brains Erasmus+ project website.
legal.title: Legal
legal.privacy_controller_text: FVEM — Federación Vizcaína de Empresas del Metal, coordinator of the Learning Brains project (Erasmus+ KA220-VET), acts as the data controller for personal data collected through this website.
legal.privacy_data_text: We only collect data that you voluntarily provide through the contact form: name, email address, and message. No data from minors or sensitive data are collected.
legal.privacy_purpose_text: Data is used exclusively to respond to project inquiries and for project dissemination activities. Data is not shared with third parties unless required by law.
legal.privacy_rights_text: You can exercise your rights of access, rectification, erasure, and objection by writing to info@fvem.es. If you consider that the processing is not compliant, you can file a complaint with your national data protection authority.
legal.privacy_legal_basis_text: Data processing is based on the legitimate interest of the consortium for the management and dissemination of the project (Art. 6.1.f GDPR) and on your consent when requested (Art. 6.1.a GDPR).
legal.privacy_retention_text: Data will be kept for the duration of the project (until 2027) and subsequently for the minimum period required by applicable law or audit requirements of the European Commission (usually 5 years after project completion).
legal.privacy_minors_text: This website is not intended for persons under 18 years of age and we do not knowingly collect personal data from minors. If we detect that data from a minor has been collected without parental consent, we will delete it immediately.
legal.cookies_what_text: Cookies are small files stored in your browser to improve the browsing experience. This site uses only technical cookies necessary for the operation of the site.
legal.cookies_types_text: Session cookies: for navigation between pages and the language selector. Google Analytics: to measure traffic anonymously (if enabled). No advertising or third-party tracking cookies are used.
legal.cookies_manage_text: You can disable or delete cookies in your browser settings. Please note that some website functions may stop working correctly if you block all cookies.
legal.cookies_analytics_text: This site may use Google Analytics to measure traffic anonymously. Analytical cookies are only activated if you accept the consent banner. If you decline, no personal data is saved.
legal.eu_disclaimer: Co-funded by the Erasmus+ Programme of the European Union. This project has been funded with support from the European Commission. This publication [communication] reflects the views only of the author, and the Commission cannot be held responsible for any use which may be made of the information contained therein.

cookies_banner.text: We use necessary technical cookies and, if you accept, analytical cookies (Google Analytics) to improve your experience.

newsletter.mobile_hint: View or download the official PDF version of our newsletter on your mobile device.
newsletter.fallback_desc: Your browser does not support inline PDF viewing or is configured to download files. You can download the PDF to view it on your device.
